narrative:
The market is operating in a business as usual regime, with a stress score of 23 out of 100 confirming a calm backdrop. Equity returns are modestly positive over the past five days, the z-score on SPY sits at a benign 0.40, and there is no meaningful sign of systemic strain keeping conditions well within normal bounds.

At 16.3, the VIX sits at the 30th percentile of its ten-year range and the term structure is in contango, both consistent with an orderly, risk-tolerant environment. Credit is emphatically confirming this picture: high-yield spreads at 271 basis points are at just the 8th percentile of their five-year range with a z-score of negative 1.12, and investment-grade spreads at 73 basis points carry a z-score of negative 1.28, meaning credit is historically tight and not pricing any meaningful stress whatsoever.

Energy stands out as the clear sectoral laggard, with XLE down 7.0 percent over the past five days, a move that diverges sharply from the broad tape. On the other side, XLK has gained 6.5 percent and EEM is up 6.4 percent, pointing to a rotation dynamic where technology and emerging market exposure are attracting capital at the expense of commodities and energy names.
